title: SQL as a Calculator
description: Do basic math directly in your query.

slides.title: Math in query! ­¤¦«
slides.content:
# Basic Arithmetic

Your database is very good at math. You can perform calculations directly in your `SELECT` statements using standard math symbols!

`+` (Add), `-` (Subtract), `*` (Multiply), `/` (Divide).

slides.title: On-The-Fly Columns ­¤ÜĆ
slides.content:
## Example Time

Imagine you have a `products` table with strings and their `price`. You want to see the price of everything if you bought two of them.

`SELECT name, price * 2 FROM products;`

The database effortlessly creates a *fake* magical column right in your result containing the doubled price!

quizData.question: If you want to see the price minus a $5 discount, how would you write the calculation?
quizData.explanation: You can just use standard mathematical operators directly on the column variable name.

slides.title: Combining with AS ­¤ÄŁ
slides.content:
## Nicknaming Math

When you do math, the column name usually looks awful (like `price * 2`). You should use `AS` to clean it up!

`SELECT price * 2 AS double_price FROM products;`

This makes your results significantly more professional.

quizData.question: Why is it best practice to use `AS` when doing math in a SELECT query?

options.text: Without it, the database will crash
It renames the resulting ugly mathematical column into something readable

quizData.explanation: Math columns get messy names by default. Utilizing AS ensures your output grid has clean, professional headers.

slides.title: Try it!
slides.content: Calculate the annual salary of employees. We only have their monthly salary. Name the result column `yearly_salary`.
slides.codeSnippet:
SELECT name, monthly_salary * 12 AS yearly_salary
FROM employees;
